# Forkjoin Agentic Tooling — Gap-Coverage Audit

Generated by a 61-agent Workflow run judging all 320 stories from the [five-perspective user-story set](agent-tooling-user-stories-superset-by-area.md) against the actual state of this repo — not the stories in the abstract, but grepped, read, and cited against real files. Each area got 2-6 independent research agents (sized to story count) followed by one synthesis agent; verdicts are MET / PARTIAL / MISSING / UNCLEAR.

## Global verdict tally

| Verdict | Count | Share |
|---|---:|---:|
| MET | 51 | 15% |
| PARTIAL | 176 | 55% |
| MISSING | 92 | 28% |
| UNCLEAR | 1 | 0% |
| **Total** | **320** | |

# Executive Summary — Forkjoin Agentic Tooling Gap-Coverage Audit

## Overall maturity

Forkjoin's agentic tooling is **deep but not closed-loop**: point engineering is frequently excellent — UCAN capability scoping, fail-closed auth in gnosis, real retry/backoff and multipart-rollback logic in x-ranger and a0, a working `.gg` FORK/FOLD DSL, a genuinely fast `network/search` over 505 tools, and durable cross-session memory — but almost every mature mechanism is scoped to one subsystem and never generalized to the actual chokepoint an agent or operator hits day to day. The pattern repeats across all 13 areas: a safety, cost, or reliability primitive exists somewhere in the tree, and the canonical path (`a0 xr deploy`, the MCP `tools/call` dispatch, `.claude/settings.json`) routes around it. The result is a platform that is instruction-honest (AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md are detailed and largely accurate) but enforcement-poor: safety and governance currently depend on the model reading and complying with prose, not on the system being structurally unable to proceed without a gate.

## Highest-priority gaps, ranked by blast-radius reduction

1. **Zero PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks exist anywhere in the repo.** This is the single root-cause lever — it's the reason destructive-command confirmation, secret-leak detection, sovereign-tooling enforcement, and permission scoping all remain prose-only rather than technical gates (Areas 6, 8, 11, 12, 13).
2. **The sovereign-tooling mandate has no mechanical enforcement and has already been violated in production** — a disabled cloud routine ran raw `wrangler deploy` undetected until manual discovery, and `a0 xr deploy` itself skips the cost/dependency guardrails that `a0 forge deploy` enforces (Areas 7, 8, 9, 11, 12, 13).
3. **`.claude/settings.local.json` grants blanket, unscoped `Bash`/`Agent`/`Task`/`WebFetch` permissions**, making every downstream confirmation convention (B5 "ask about destruction," type-to-confirm deploy gates) moot for whole classes of action (Areas 6, 11, 12).
4. **No cap on concurrent or total spawned agent subprocesses** — `fireCannonLanes` (a0's real production repair-spawn path) has no concurrency semaphore and no total ceiling, despite a capped fork primitive already existing elsewhere in the codebase (Area 9).
5. **Safety/side-effect metadata (`destructiveHint`, `readOnlyHint`) is authored correctly at 167/170 leaf tools but stripped at the MCP aggregation boundary**, so the agent-facing search/docs/directory surface never sees it (Area 1).
6. **No LLM token/dollar budget tracking anywhere** — only Cloudflare infra cost is measured; two real incidents (~$2K GPU drain, ~1000 paid browser-hours) were discovered via invoice, not telemetry, and CLAUDE.md's default workflow actively biases toward the most expensive fanout posture with no accompanying check (Area 7).
7. **No crash/resume path and no `PreCompact` hook** — continuity survives only as an incidental side effect of harness features the repo happens to sit on, not by repo-native design, despite the data (`events.jsonl`, `qdoc` snapshots) already existing to drive one (Area 5).
8. **"Never stop until complete" is written as an unconditional guardrail**, directly undercutting legitimate stop-on-command, ask-when-blocked, and plan-before-acting behaviors — one prose fix resolves three separate MISSING findings (Area 11).
9. **No retryable/idempotency signal crosses the MCP protocol boundary** — mature 4xx/5xx retry discrimination and rollback logic exist deep in `r2.ts`/`search-console.ts` but flatten to an undifferentiated `-32603` the moment an agent actually calls a tool (Areas 3, 4).

## Cross-cutting theme

The dominant pattern across nearly all 13 areas is **"the primitive exists in one place and isn't wired to the chokepoint that matters."** Safety, cost, reliability, and versioning mechanisms are consistently built once, correctly, in a narrow subsystem (a single CLI command, a single tool file, a single incident-response script) and never promoted to a shared library or enforced at the actual agent/tool/deploy boundary. Closing this one architectural habit — generalize-and-wire rather than build-narrow-and-move-on — would resolve a majority of the PARTIAL findings in this audit without new invention.

---

## Per-area detail

### 1. Discoverability, Naming & Documentation

## Overall read

This area is a split picture: the *discovery* mechanics (search, canonical naming, docs-drift testing) are genuinely solid, but the *safety/disambiguation metadata* layer is almost entirely absent at the surface agents actually use. `apps/mcp-superserver` has real infrastructure — fuzzy `network/search` over 505 tools (Agent #3), enforced canonical-name uniqueness with a CI-style gate (Tool #10), and a smoke test that keeps published docs in sync with the live registry (Tool #13). But none of that infrastructure carries structured side-effect metadata through: 167/170 leaf `apps/*-mcp/src/tools.ts` files declare `readOnlyHint`/`destructiveHint`/`openWorldHint` correctly, and that data is thrown away at the aggregation boundary (`toNetworkTool()` in `registry.ts:1237`, `serializeTool()` in `runtime.ts:968`) before it ever reaches `network/search`, `network/docs`, `network/directory`, or `tools/list`. The larger `aic-tools.generated.ts` surface (505 tools, including destructive ones like `dash_studio_delete_kv_key`) never had structured annotations in the first place. Cross-referencing between near-duplicate tools (the `get_emotion_*` cluster), tool-level deprecation, protocol-level `instructions`, and batch schema fetch are all either missing or half-built. Net: strong bones, no safety skin.

## Verdict tally

| Verdict | Count |
|---|---|
| MET | 3 |
| PARTIAL | 11 |
| MISSING | 4 |
| UNCLEAR | 0 |
| **Total** | **18** |

## Prioritized punch list

1. **Annotations exist at the leaf but are stripped by the aggregation layer** (Agent #6, Tool #15 — PARTIAL). 167/170 `apps/*-mcp/src/tools.ts` files already declare `readOnlyHint`/`destructiveHint`/`openWorldHint`, but `toNetworkTool()` (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/registry.ts:1237-1262`) and `serializeTool()` (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts:968-997`) only copy `x-forkjoin` metadata through, dropping `tool.annotations` entirely — so the canonical agent-facing path (`network/search`/`docs`/`directory`/`tools/list`) never surfaces safety hints that already exist. **Fix first**: copy `tool.annotations` through in `toNetworkTool()` and `serializeTool()`, and add `readOnlyHint: true` to the superserver's own `network/*` built-ins (all currently read-only, all currently annotation-less). This is the highest-leverage item because the data already exists — it's a plumbing fix, not new authoring work, and it fixes both #6 and #15 at once.

2. **No structured annotations on the larger `aic-tools.generated.ts` surface at all** (Tool #11 — PARTIAL). 505 tools including destructive-sounding ones (`dash_studio_delete_kv_key`, `dash_studio_delete_r2_object`) have zero `annotations`/`readOnlyHint`/`destructiveHint` occurrences — side-effect character is implied only by name/prose. Port the `readOnlyAnnotations` pattern already used correctly in `open-source/a0/src/mcp/tools.ts` into the generator producing `aic-tools.generated.ts`, auto-deriving hints from verb prefix (`get_`/`list_` → read-only, `delete_`/`revoke_`/`set_` → destructive) with a manual override list for edge cases.

3. **Side-effect disclosure is incidental free text, not a designed contract** (Agent #1 — PARTIAL). Only ~1% of the 1233 tool descriptions mention read-only/destructive language at all, and `grep -rl 'readOnlyHint|destructiveHint|idempotentHint|openWorldHint' apps/mcp-superserver/src/*.ts` returns nothing — confirming #2 above isn't just missing data but a missing *mechanism*. Add the MCP spec's own annotation fields to the generation pipeline (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/generate-aic-tools.ts`), and add a `network/publication_audit` invariant (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/publication-report.ts`) that fails the gate for any `create_/delete_/set_/revoke_`-prefixed tool missing an annotation — this turns items 1-3 into one durable, self-enforcing contract instead of three one-off patches.

4. **No `instructions` field on `initialize`** (Runtime #20 — MISSING). Neither `apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts`'s initialize handler (~line 1778) nor `open-source/a0/src/mcp/index.ts` populates the protocol's `instructions` field — and the underlying `open-source/mcp-framework/` SDK doesn't even wire the capability up. All equivalent guidance lives out-of-band in AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md prose, invisible to any bare MCP client. Add `instructions` (sourced from the same "Superserver MCP Tooling Tutorial" text already synced via `a0sync`) to the `InitializeResult` in `runtime.ts`'s `case 'initialize'`, and add framework-level support in `open-source/mcp-framework/src/server.ts` so `a0`'s server can pass one too. High leverage because it fixes onboarding for *every* client, not just this repo's own agents who already read AGENTS.md.

5. **No tool-level deprecation field or policy** (Tool #12 — MISSING). Zero matches for `@deprecated`/`DEPRECATED`/`isDeprecated` across ~170 `apps/*-mcp/src/tools.ts` files, and no deprecation notice pattern anywhere in the superserver's docs. Add `deprecated?: { since; replacement; removeAfter }` to the shared `Tool` type in `open-source/mcp-framework/src/types.ts`, surface it in `serializeTool()`, and have `publication-report.ts` flag tools nearing `removeAfter`.

6. **Deprecated/removed surfaces give no pointer to their replacement** (Agent #8 — MISSING). Concrete live case: `a0 github mirror` was removed (replaced by `a0 gn flow --github-writes` per user memory), but `open-source/a0/src/workspace.ts:510`'s `resolveProject()` throws a bare `Unable to resolve project 'mirror'` with no hint. Add a deprecation-alias map to `resolveProject()` so removed/renamed targets throw a named-replacement error, and require the same pattern for any MCP tool retired from `aic-tools.generated.ts` — this pairs naturally with item 5's schema field.

7. **No "read before calling" / scope-limiting convention** (Tool #9 — MISSING). Neither AGENTS.md nor CLAUDE.md instructs agents to consult descriptions before calling, and only 4 of ~170 `apps/*-mcp/src/tools.ts` files have any scope-limiting phrasing, none of it real "I don't support X" disambiguation. Add a "Not for: ..." convention requirement to AGENTS.md and a lint/generator check in the superserver's tool-audit pipeline flagging descriptions missing a scope-limiting clause.

8. **No batch schema-fetch API for named tools** (Runtime #22 — PARTIAL). `network/docs` (`buildDocsPayload`, `runtime.ts:1222-1276`) only takes single-string substring filters (`server`/`tool`/`tag`); an agent that knows 3 specific tool names must either pull the whole catalog or make repeated calls. Add a `tools: string[]` exact-name array parameter to `network/docs`'s input schema, matched with OR logic.

9. **`tools/list` eagerly dumps the entire ~1233-tool set with no deferred/lazy model** (Agent #4 — PARTIAL), compounding item 8: there is no repo-documented equivalent of the host harness's own `select:A,B,C` batch-fetch pattern. Convert the superserver's own `tools/list` to a deferred/searchable model paired with the `network/docs` batch param from item 8.

10. **No tool-level `seeAlso`/cross-referencing, only server-level** (Agent #7 — PARTIAL). `registry.ts`'s `relatedServers` array covers only 11 of 128 backends and operates at server granularity; individual tool descriptions have almost no "use this when / see also" language (4 hits across 1233 descriptions, none an explicit tool-to-tool pointer). Add a `seeAlso: string[]` field to the tool-generation schema (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/generate-aic-tools.ts`) and surface it through `network/docs`.

11. **Near-duplicate tool cluster has no disambiguation** (Agent #2 — PARTIAL). The `get_emotion_*` family (7 tools: clusters, time_series, territories, statistics, graph_analysis, related_emotions) each has a solid standalone description but zero cross-links or "use this over X when..." framing. Add that clause per tool (or fold into item 10's `seeAlso` field) and require it as a lint rule whenever a new tool name shares >70% token-overlap with an existing one.

12. **No cross-surface (CLI/MCP/SDK) naming registry** (Tool #16 — PARTIAL). The same tool changes textual form across transports (`network/search` HTTP vs. `network__search` stdio via `scripts/mcp-superserver-stdio.mjs:148-162`), and `open-source/a0` has no `network` CLI subcommand at all, so there's nothing to be consistent *with*. Either give `a0` wrapper subcommands reusing each `canonicalName`, or promote the `/` ↔ `__` transport mapping from AGENTS.md prose into the canonical-name contract itself.

13. **Examples exist on ~1% of tools, no bad-call/anti-examples anywhere** (Agent #5 — PARTIAL). Only 6 of 505 tools in `aic-tools.generated.ts` have an `Examples:` block, and zero tools anywhere demonstrate an incorrect call to contrast against. Extend the documented example template to a generator-enforced requirement, including a required "Do NOT call with X when Y — use `<other_tool>` instead" line.

14. **Examples aren't validated against actual input schemas** (Tool #13 — PARTIAL). `publication-contract.smoke.ts` checks doc/registry structural drift (names, namespaces, endpoints) but never inspects the hand-authored `Examples:` prose against each tool's `inputSchema` — so examples could silently drift out of sync with real parameters. Add a sibling smoke test that extracts `Examples:` snippets and validates argument shape against the JSON schema, failing the publication gate on mismatch.

### 2. Contracts, Validation & Invocation

## Overall read

The production-facing tool surface (`apps/mcp-server/src/tools/*.ts`, ~74 files) has a real, consistently-applied validation convention: explicit `McpError(ErrorCode.InvalidParams, ...)` rejections naming the missing/malformed field (269 occurrences), documented defaults in schema descriptions (`(default: N)`), and a handful of tools that state and enforce real size ceilings (`llm-inference.ts`, `social.ts`, `microcontracts.ts`). That discipline does not carry over to the newer `apps/mcp-superserver` surface, which hand-rolls its own `tools/call` dispatch with no schema-validation library anywhere in the repo (no ajv, no zod, no hand-rolled JSON-Schema check — confirmed by grep) and no contract test asserting that a declared `inputSchema` actually matches handler behavior. The result is a two-tier system: mature, self-enforcing contracts on the old surface, and prose-only/silently-coercing contracts on several native superserver tools (`network/workflow_get`, `network/workflow_validate`), plus scattered one-off gaps (missing enums, no dry-run, undocumented path semantics, no universal size/unit conventions) that a lint or generic validator would catch structurally rather than case by case.

## Verdict tally

- MET: 1
- PARTIAL: 14
- MISSING: 3
- UNCLEAR: 0
- Total findings: 18

## Prioritized punch list

**1. No generic schema-validation step in the superserver's `tools/call` dispatch** (Runtime #18 – MISSING; Tool #1, Tool #2, Agent #13 – PARTIAL, same root cause)
`apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts` implements `tools/call` by hand with no ajv/zod/jsonschema anywhere in the repo. Required-field enforcement is hand-written per case (`read_file`/`write_file` throw `'path is required'`), so schema and code can silently drift — and native handlers `network/workflow_get` (~1942-1948) and `network/workflow_validate` (~1950-1953) prove the drift already happened: both coerce a missing *required* field (`workflowId`, `topologySource`) to `null`/`''` instead of rejecting it. Fix once, closes four findings at once: add one generic validator in the `tools/call` handler that checks `args` against each `builtin.inputSchema` (types + `required`) before dispatch, emitting a uniform `-32602` error naming the field — reusing the `McpError` pattern already proven in `apps/mcp-server/src/tools/delegation.ts`. Pair it with a contract test (parallel to `apps/mcp-superserver/src/publication-contract.smoke.ts`) that synthesizes valid/invalid calls per declared schema and fails CI if schema and handler diverge.

**2. No platform-level input-size contract** (Tool #5 – PARTIAL; Agent #16 – PARTIAL, same gap)
Only 3-4 tools (`llm-inference.ts` 50k-char cap, `social.ts` 280-char cap, `microcontracts.ts` 100/500-char caps) state and enforce a real limit; the generated catalog otherwise encodes `"maxLength": null` for nearly every string property, and there's no body-size guard anywhere in the `/mcp` JSON-RPC route in `runtime.ts`. Worse, `local/read_file` (~1134-1139) silently truncates oversized reads (`truncated: bytes.byteLength > maxBytes`) rather than rejecting cleanly. Add a body-size guard at the `/mcp` route entry (413 / `-32600` on oversized requests), decide and document whether `read_file` truncation is intentional, and add a registry-level lint (in `apps/mcp-superserver/src/registry.ts` or `generate-aic-tools.ts`) flagging `maxLength: null` string properties with no size language in their description.

**3. No dry-run/preview on side-effecting local tools** (Agent #12 – PARTIAL)
The a0 CLI has broad `--dry-run` support (`a0 clean`, `a0 install`, `a0 docker build`, `a0 seo verify/submit`, `a0 control reconcile`, `a0 xr deploy` forwarding `--dry-run` to `wrangler deploy`), but the superserver's own `write_file`/`replace_in_file` (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts` ~1169-1214) write immediately with no preview parameter. Add a `dryRun: boolean` param that returns the would-be content/diff without touching disk, matching the established a0 CLI pattern.

**4. Path-parameter contract undocumented vs. actual behavior** (Tool #4 – MISSING; Agent #14 – PARTIAL, same root cause)
`resolveLocalPath()` (`runtime.ts:1044-1062`) and `resolveStatusLogPath()` (`workflows.ts:193-195`) both use `path.resolve(root, input)`, which natively accepts absolute paths and only rejects them if they land outside the workspace root — so absolute paths already work today. But every exposed schema description (`list_directory`/`read_file`/`search_files`/`write_file`/`replace_in_file`, lines 902/915/928/942/956) says only `'Path relative to the monorepo root'`, with no mention absolute paths are accepted (and validated). Update those descriptions to state the true contract explicitly: `'absolute or relative to the monorepo root; absolute paths outside the root are rejected'`.

**5. Enum convention not applied uniformly for closed-set string params** (Agent #9 – PARTIAL)
Enums are used correctly in several places (`skymesh-tools.ts:518,545,567/594`, `runtime.ts:846`), but `apps/mcp-superserver/src/forgo-trd-tools.ts`'s `target` param (lines 51-55, 88) lists its seven valid runtime targets only in prose as plain `type: 'string'`, so an invalid value passes schema-shape checks and fails only deep in execution. Add `enum: ['agnostic','workers','node','bun','gnode','wasm-browser','wasi']` to `target`, and audit other `type: 'string'` params whose description lists a closed option set.

**6. No convention for documenting tool ordering/precondition dependencies** (Tool #3 – MISSING)
No repo-wide pattern exists for stating "call X before Y" — `write_file`/`replace_in_file` do their own defensive re-checks instead of declaring a precondition, and the only related prose hit (`aic-tools.generated.ts:634`, session-status tool) is a single incidental description, not an enforced convention. Where a real ordering dependency exists (e.g. `replace_in_file` assumes the file exists and was recently read), add an explicit `Preconditions:` line to the tool description, applied consistently across `runtime.ts`'s local tools and the generated catalog.

**7. Object-typed param whose description invites a stringified-JSON call** (Agent #11 – PARTIAL)
`nexus_zk_decrypt_key_envelope`'s `envelope` param (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/aic-tools.generated.ts:11611-11613`) is typed `object` but its description says `'NexusKeyEnvelope object or JSON string'`. Fix the tool to require a structured object only, and add a registration-time lint (in `generate-aic-tools.ts`) flagging any `inputSchema` property whose description mentions "JSON string"/"stringified" on a non-string type.

**8. Documented ordering invariant with no enforcement** (Tool #7 – PARTIAL)
`apps/mcp-superserver/README.md:22` states `'Queue gate: call network/publication_audit before adding or publishing MCP backend work'`, but no code path in `runtime.ts` gates add/publish on that call having run — it's prose-only. Either add a lightweight precondition (the add/publish path self-invokes `publication_audit` or checks a session-scoped flag it sets), or update the README to say explicitly that compliance is agent-trust-only and unenforced.

**9. Timestamp/relative-time ambiguity** (Agent #38 – PARTIAL)
Some fields document ISO 8601 correctly (`aic-tools.generated.ts:3965-3967, 5521`), but the same timer tool also accepts `relativeTime` (`'Relative time like "2 hours"'`, lines 3969-3971) — the exact ambiguity class the convention warns against — and `afterTimestamp` (lines 801-804) is a bare `type: number` with no stated epoch unit. Standardize on ISO 8601 with explicit offset/Z for all `*At`/`*Timestamp` string fields, rename/annotate bare numeric timestamps (e.g. `afterTimestamp` → `afterTimestampMs`), and either resolve `relativeTime` server-side into an absolute timestamp or drop it from the agent-facing schema.

**10. Unit suffixes not universal on numeric params** (Tool #6 – PARTIAL)
Broadly followed (`visibleMs`, `staleAfterMinutes`, `maxBytes`/`maxEntries`, `durationSeconds`/`ttlSeconds`/`delayHours` etc.) but not universal: `afterTimestamp` has no stated epoch unit, and `viewportWidth`/`viewportHeight` (`skymesh-tools.ts:548-549`) have no unit at all. Add a generation-time check (in `generate-aic-tools.ts` or a registry test) flagging `type: number` properties whose name lacks a unit suffix and whose description doesn't state one.

**11. Parameter naming diverges for the same concept within one tool family** (Agent #15 – PARTIAL)
`polyglot-scanner-tools.ts`'s `scan`/`get_scan_result` use `repo` (lines 11-13, 35-38) while `create_pr` in the same file uses `repoFullName` (line 76) for the identical concept. Rename to converge on one name, and add a cheap CI check flagging near-duplicate-concept parameter names diverging within one backend's tool set.

**12. Optional-param defaults undocumented on local dev tools** (Agent #10 – PARTIAL)
`apps/mcp-server/src/tools/*.ts` consistently documents defaults in schema (`'(default: 50)'` matching the real runtime fallback), but `runtime.ts`'s `buildLocalTools()` (`local/list_directory`, `local/read_file`, `local/search_files`, ~lines 894-965) declares `maxEntries`/`maxBytes`/`maxMatches` as bare `{ type: 'number' }` even though `numberArg()` silently applies 200/200_000/100 fallbacks. Add `(default: N)` language to match the established convention.

### 3. Errors, Retries & Reliability

## Overall read

Error handling in this repo is a tale of two layers. Deep inside specific tool implementations — `open-source/x-ranger/src/r2.ts`, `open-source/a0/src/search-console.ts`, `open-source/a0/src/seo.ts`, `open-source/gnosis/src/runtime/structured-concurrency.ts`, `open-source/aeon-pipelines/src/fold.ts` — the reliability engineering is genuinely mature: 4xx-vs-5xx retry discrimination, exponential backoff, multipart rollback with compound error messages, `Promise.allSettled` fault isolation, real `AbortController` cancellation, and rule-coded actionable errors with `Fix:` strings all exist and are exercised in production paths (`a0 xr deploy`, `gn flow`, mesh folds).

None of that maturity has crossed the boundary into `apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts`'s `tools/call` dispatch — the actual surface an agent talks to. That file has exactly one internal `catch` block across 2000+ lines, and it flattens every failure into `{code: -32603, message}` with no `retryable`/`transient` flag, no rollback/partial-state reporting, no rate-limit enforcement (the `discoveryRateLimited`/`documentationRateLimited` flags are set but never enforced), no cancellation wiring, and no job-id/polling for long calls outside the workflow subsystem. So the story's reliability guarantees are true of the sovereign toolchain's internals but not yet true of the protocol contract an agent actually receives. This is a single architectural gap (the MCP superserver error/response contract) that, if closed, would resolve roughly two-thirds of the findings below at once.

## Verdict tally

| Verdict | Count |
|---|---|
| MET | 3 |
| PARTIAL | 13 |
| MISSING | 4 |
| UNCLEAR | 0 |
| **Total** | **20** |

MET: Agent #23 (rule-coded deploy-blocking violations), Agent #64 / Runtime #63 (fork/fold `Promise.allSettled` isolation, same underlying mechanism reported twice).

## Prioritized punch list

1. **No retryable/transient signal on MCP tool errors** (Agent #18, Tool #19, Tool #22 — three independent findings converging on the same root cause). `apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts`'s `tools/call` dispatch and `proxyBackendRequest` (~lines 1841-1861, 2030-2068) return a flat `{code: -32603, message}` regardless of whether the failure was a permanent 4xx or a transient 5xx/network error, even though this exact distinction already lives inside `open-source/x-ranger/src/r2.ts` `fetchWithRetry` and `open-source/a0/src/search-console.ts`. **Fix**: surface `{code, retryable: boolean, retryAfterMs?}` on every MCP tool-call error, derived from the existing r2.ts/search-console.ts 4xx/5xx convention, and add a line to AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md telling agents to stop retrying once a response is marked non-retryable. This single change is the highest-leverage fix in the area — it's the load-bearing gap behind three separate findings.

2. **MCP superserver's own outbound fetch has no retry/backoff at all** (Agent #30). `apps/mcp-superserver/src/skymesh-tools.ts`'s `fetchJson` (lines 172-199) wraps `fetch` in a bare try/catch with zero retry logic, while the a0 CLI's `fetchWithRetry` pattern (`r2.ts` lines 184-222) is explicitly documented elsewhere as "the pattern used elsewhere in the sovereign toolchain." **Fix**: extract `fetchWithRetry` into a shared utility and apply it to `skymesh-tools.ts` and any other MCP tool doing outbound HTTP, not just the a0 CLI's Google API clients.

3. **No rollback/partial-state reporting outside r2.ts** (Agent #20). The multipart abort-on-failure + compound error message pattern in `r2.ts` (~lines 447-486) has no equivalent in `apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts`'s `tools/call` handler or `open-source/a0/src/cli.ts`'s mutating commands — a partially-executed composite tool call gives no indication of what state was left behind. **Fix**: extract a `withRollback(fn, rollback)` helper into shared-utils and apply it to multi-step mutating tools (cron create/update, control-worker task ops).

4. **Actionable-error `{rule, message, fix}` shape is confined to a0 CLI diagnostics** (Agent #17). `open-source/a0/src/cloudflare-cost.ts` and `forge.ts:956` do this well, but `open-source/x-ranger/src/r2.ts` throws bare messages and most `apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts` tool errors just relay the raw message via `mcpError(-32603, message)`. **Fix**: extract the shape into `shared-utils/src/actionable-error.ts` and require MCP tool handlers and r2.ts's thrown errors to attach a `fix`/`nextStep` string before reaching `mcpError`/`console.error`.

5. **Rate limiting is bespoke and unenforced at the MCP boundary** (Tool #18). `open-source/a0/src/seo.ts` self-throttles via `SPACING_MS = 7000`, but `apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts` only sets static, never-enforced `discoveryRateLimited: false`/`documentationRateLimited: false` flags (lines 1634-1795). **Fix**: build a shared token-bucket/pacing utility generalizing `seo.ts`'s approach and wire it into `callLocalTool`/`tools-call` dispatch so any tool can declare and enforce a per-minute budget.

6. **Retry-After header is never parsed or honored** (Tool #24). `open-source/a0/src/seo.ts`/`search-console.ts` detect 429s but back off on a hardcoded schedule (`Math.min(1000 * 2 ** attempt, 60_000)`) rather than reading the server's actual `Retry-After`/quota-reset header. **Fix**: parse `Retry-After` in `search-console.ts`'s 429 handler, use it as the backoff delay, and surface it as `retryAfterMs` on `SearchConsoleError` so it can eventually propagate to MCP tool responses.

7. **No cancellation wiring at the MCP boundary** (Tool #21). `open-source/gnosis/src/runtime/structured-concurrency.ts` has real `AbortController`-based cancellation used by `engine.ts`/`polyglot-compose.ts`, but grepping `apps/mcp-superserver/src` for `AbortController`/`AbortSignal`/`notifications/cancelled` returns nothing — an abandoned long-running MCP tool call cannot be cancelled. **Fix**: thread an `AbortSignal` through `runtime.ts`'s `tools/call` dispatch and implement MCP's `notifications/cancelled` to abort the corresponding invocation via gnosis's existing plumbing.

8. **No job-id/polling for long tool calls outside the workflow subsystem** (Agent #24). `apps/mcp-superserver/src/workflows.ts` gives `workflow_run`/`workflow_history`/`workflow_get` polling backed by `open-source/aeon-workflow/src/manager.ts`, but any other long call (`proxyBackendRequest`, ~lines 2030-2068) is a single synchronous request/response with no async handle. **Fix**: let `proxyBackendRequest` optionally return a `jobId` for calls flagged async, backed by the same `ForgeWorkflowManager` run-status store.

9. **Stack traces are either absent or dumped raw, no shared "actionable line" extraction** (Agent #21). `open-source/gnosis/src/polyglot-trace.ts`/`subprocess-invoker.ts`'s `MappedError` does real traceback-to-location mapping, but it's scoped to cross-language subprocess execution; `runtime.ts` surfaces only `error.message`, while `gnosis/src/testing/runner.ts` (~lines 2043-2046, 2265) dumps the *full* `error.stack`, the opposite failure mode. **Fix**: generalize `MappedError`/`polyglot-trace` into a shared formatter used by both the MCP superserver's error path and `runner.ts`, replacing the raw stack dump with `stack.split('\n')[0]` plus mapped source location.

10. **`a0 flow`/`gn flow` captures per-repo errors but never prints them** (Agent #19). `open-source/gnit/src/flow.rs`'s `flow()` correctly isolates per-repo failures (`result.errors.push((label, e)); continue;`, lines 203/247/249) but the CLI only prints an aggregate count (lines 347-358) — the calling agent sees "N errors" with no indication of which repo or why. **Fix**: print `result.errors` in the flow summary, or add `a0 flow --json` returning the full `FlowResult` so an agent can programmatically retry only the failed items.

11. **No batch tool returns per-item outcomes** (Tool #20). No MCP tool accepts array input with a structured per-item results array; the closest analog, `gnit/src/flow.rs`'s per-repo tracking, only reports aggregates (see #10). **Fix**: for any future batch MCP tool, return `results: [{item, ok, error?}]`; retrofit `a0 flow`'s output to expose its already-collected per-repo results this way.

12. **No idempotency-key support on the tools an agent actually calls** (Tool #17). Idempotency keys are a real, used pattern in business logic (`open-source/aeon-voting/src/exchange.ts` line 340, `dashrelay-app`'s D1 unique index), but zero handling exists in `apps/mcp-superserver/src/*.ts`, `open-source/a0/src/cli.ts`, or `gnit/src/flow.rs`'s commit/publish path. **Fix**: add an optional `idempotencyKey` argument to MCP write tools and `a0 flow`/`gn flow`, following the `exchange.ts` pattern.

13. **`/search` route conflates "no query" with "zero matches" with "error swallowed"** (Agent #39). Unlike `a0 logs`'s explicit `payload.error && entries.length === 0` distinction (`cli.ts:4065`), `apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts:1547`'s search route returns `results: []` identically in all three cases. **Fix**: add an explicit `matchedNothing: boolean` / `error: string|null` field to the `/search` response and other MCP query routes, mirroring the `a0 logs` convention.

14. **Arg-validation helpers silently coalesce wrong-type to undefined** (Agent #22). `apps/mcp-superserver/src/skymesh-tools.ts`'s `readString`/`readNumber`/`readBoolean` (lines 27-51) return `undefined` on type mismatch instead of distinguishing "absent" from "present but wrong type," unlike the a0 CLI's loud `throw new Error('Unsupported ...')` pattern at the subcommand layer. **Fix**: throw an explicit validation error for type mismatches instead of silently coalescing to undefined.

15. **No adversarial/fuzz testing of MCP tool inputs** (Tool #23). `apps/mcp-superserver/src/__tests__` has only 4 files / 10 test cases, none targeting malformed or adversarial tool input, and no CI workflow runs fuzz/chaos testing against tool inputs. **Fix**: add an adversarial/fuzz test suite (malformed JSON args, wrong types, oversized payloads, injected control characters) gated in CI before a tool is registered/deployed.

### 4. State, Concurrency & Side Effects

## Overall read

State/concurrency discipline in this repo is bimodal: where a single subsystem owns its own consistency story end-to-end (monster-swarm's `FleetLock` + `DECAGON=10` population ceiling, a0's control-plane `upsert*` merge-by-key writers, a0's dry-run/`--confirm=` apply gate, gnosis's content-hash scan cache, a0's stale-lease reclaim in `cli.ts`), it is genuinely solid and tested (`tests/paris_subagent.rs`, `scripts/demo-autofork.sh`). But every one of those is a bespoke, subsystem-local solution. The moment you cross into the actual agent-tool boundary — `apps/mcp-superserver`'s generated catalog of 1000s of MCP tools — the cupboard is close to bare: no idempotency keys on retried writes, no global concurrency cap, no MCP-protocol cancellation, no progress/heartbeat channel, no optimistic-concurrency enforcement on writes despite a `version` field existing in the type system. The repo has proven the *patterns* it needs (FleetLock, upsert-by-key, dry-run/apply split, content-hash cache invalidation) in one or two places each; the punch list below is almost entirely "generalize this pattern to the MCP tool surface," not "invent a new pattern."

## Verdict tally

| Verdict | Count |
|---|---|
| MET | 7 |
| PARTIAL | 12 |
| MISSING | 3 |
| UNCLEAR | 1 |
| **Total** | **23** |

MET: Runtime #35, #38, #39, #40, Agent #26, Tool #27, Tool #32
MISSING: Agent #25, Runtime #34, Runtime #37
PARTIAL: Agent #27, #28, #29, #31, Tool #25, #26, #28, #29, #30, #31, Runtime #33, #36
UNCLEAR: Runtime #21

## Prioritized punch list

Ordered by how much closing each would move the needle, not by tag number.

1. **No retry-safe writes at the agent-tool boundary (Agent #25, MISSING).** Zero hits for `idempotencyKey`/`Idempotency-Key` anywhere outside vendored code; `apps/mcp-superserver/src/aic-tools.generated.ts`'s thousands of mutating tool defs (DASH sessions, R2 writes, D1 mutations) have no dedup key. A retried MCP call today double-applies. **Fix:** add an `idempotencyKey` parameter to mutating MCP tools, backed by a D1/KV `(tool, key)` dedup table with a short TTL, so retries short-circuit to the prior result.

2. **No global concurrency cap where agent calls actually land (Runtime #33 / Agent #28, PARTIAL).** `apps/mcp-superserver/src/worker.ts` is a thin pass-through with no throttling; the only caps that exist are opt-in (`frf.ts`'s per-call `concurrency`) or scoped to one fleet (monster-swarm's `DECAGON=10`). Cloudflare Queues' `max_concurrency` is supported by `cloudflare-worker-deploy.ts` but `grep -rl max_concurrency` over every wrangler config returns zero — the capability is unused. **Fix:** wire a default concurrency gate into `runtime.ts` using the existing `frf.ts` primitive (or actually set `max_concurrency` in a `wrangler.jsonc`), so a burst degrades into a queue instead of requiring every caller to opt in.

3. **No generic "who owns this resource" contention detector (Tool #29, PARTIAL).** The only real ownership ledger (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/ledger-tool-audit.generated.json`, checked via `network/publication_audit`) is scoped to theorem-to-server mappings, not to D1 tables/KV namespaces/R2 buckets that multiple backends might both claim to write. **Fix:** generalize the ledger-tool-audit pattern in `generate-topology-sources.ts` + `registry.ts` so `network/publication_audit` flags two backends both claiming write ownership of the same resource.

4. **No pre-edit collision warning between concurrent agents on the same file (Runtime #37, MISSING).** AGENTS.md explicitly normalizes dirty shared worktrees ("Prefer integration over isolation," B19a) and a0 only reconciles its *own* task-lease staleness (`abandoned_running_tasks`/`stale_claimed_leases` via `a0 diagnose`) — there's no analog for two live coding agents about to edit the same path. Given the repo's fork/race/fold multi-agent norm, this is a real, frequently-hit gap. **Fix:** surface a pre-edit warning through `a0 gn status` or a Claude Code hook when another active worktree has uncommitted changes touching the same path.

5. **`version` field exists but nothing enforces it on writes (Agent #31, PARTIAL).** `shared-utils/src/ontology/types.ts:60-61` documents `EntityMetadata.version?: number` for "optimistic concurrency," and R2 tools expose etag on read, but no write path compares a caller-supplied version/etag against current state — grep for `If-Match`/`expectedVersion`/412 across `apps/mcp-superserver` and `shared-backend` found nothing. **Fix:** wire the ontology write path to require/compare `version` and throw a typed 409 on mismatch; expose the same etag/version on R2 and D1/KV write tools.

6. **No generic serialize-writes contract for tools that can't self-lock (Tool #28, PARTIAL).** `monster-swarm`'s `FleetLock` (`open-source/gnosis/monster-swarm/src/store.rs`, held across every RMW in `ops.rs`) is real but is the tool defending itself, not a runtime-enforced contract for the many MCP backends (D1/KV) that have no such self-lock. **Fix:** document a "serialize writes to this resource" contract in tool descriptions and have `registry.ts`/`runtime.ts` hold a per-resource async mutex around matching tool names.

7. **No MCP-protocol-level cancellation (Agent #29, PARTIAL).** a0's own `bailController`/`mergeAbortSignals()` in `runner.ts` (~L720-800) genuinely kills child processes on abort, and `control-plane.ts` threads `abortSignal` through long calls — but none of this reaches the MCP layer; no `notifications/cancelled` handling exists in `apps/mcp-superserver` or `open-source/a0/src/mcp/*.ts`. **Fix:** wire `notifications/cancelled` into `registry.ts` + `open-source/a0/src/mcp/handler.ts` and document the surface in AGENTS.md.

8. **No ambient sandbox/dry-run signal for MCP tools (Tool #31, PARTIAL).** a0's CLI has pervasive real `--dry-run` flags, but it requires the calling agent to remember to pass it per call; no session-level sandbox context reaches `aic-tools.generated.ts`/`runtime.ts` (grep for `dryRun`/sandbox context params returns nothing). **Fix:** inject a hidden `_sandbox: boolean` from `runtime.ts` based on session state so a0-convention tools auto-suppress side effects without per-call opt-in.

9. **No session-scoped tmp directory convention in a0 itself (Runtime #34, MISSING).** `.a0/tmp` subdirectories are purpose-named (`moonshine-mesh-hotfix`, `large-qa-datasets`), not session-namespaced, so two parallel `a0` sessions can collide; only the outer Claude Code harness scratchpad is genuinely per-session. **Fix:** adopt `.a0/tmp/<session-id>/...` derived from an env var or generated UUID at session start.

10. **Stale worktrees never swept (Runtime #36, PARTIAL).** `ExitWorktree` cleanup is a manual user-facing prompt only; concrete drift observed — `.claude/worktrees/agent-a56727561e88fcfbc` sat 9+ days with real uncommitted changes and no GC job exists. **Fix:** add a periodic/session-start sweep that runs `git worktree list`, checks each `.claude/worktrees/*` entry against its base ref, and auto-removes zero-diff ones.

11. **No progress/heartbeat channel for individual MCP tool calls (Tool #26, PARTIAL).** a0's own workspace-task leases carry `heartbeatAt` and render via `a0 top --watch`/`a0 logs --live`, but grep for `progressToken`/`notifications/progress` in `apps/mcp-superserver` and `open-source/a0/src/mcp/*.ts` returns nothing — an individual long-running MCP call (verification-mcp, network/*) has no liveness signal. **Fix:** extend the lease-heartbeat pattern to MCP progress notifications keyed by `progressToken`.

12. **Rate limits are prose, not enforcement (Tool #25, PARTIAL).** Only one generated tool (`aic-tools.generated.ts:585`, DASH collab sessions) documents a concrete limit ("100 ops/sec, 5000 ops/min per editor") in its description text; nothing measures or throttles actual call rate anywhere else in the catalog. **Fix:** add a `concurrencyLimit`/`rateLimit` field to `generate-aic-tools.ts` and a per-tool call-rate tracker in `runtime.ts` that defers/queues over-limit calls.

13. **No per-tool concurrency-safety annotation (Agent #27, PARTIAL).** AGENTS.md states the fork/race/fold and "prefer integration over isolation" philosophy at the repo level, but no individual tool schema in `registry.ts` says "must-serialize" vs. "parallel-safe" — agents only get the philosophy, not per-call guidance. **Fix:** add a `Concurrency` field to the MCP tool registry schema and surface it in generated tool descriptions.

14. **No enforced session/handle-close hook (Tool #30, PARTIAL).** `mcp__claude-in-chrome__tabs_close_mcp` and `ExitWorktree`'s keep/remove prompt exist at the harness layer, but no repo-native MCP tool enforces release of a returned handle, and no `.claude/settings.json` hook checks for unclosed sessions at task end. **Fix:** add a PostToolUse/Stop hook (via the update-config skill) that checks for open tab-group tabs or unclosed sessions and auto-closes or warns.

**Note on UNCLEAR:** Runtime #21 (stale-handle/session-epoch detection) has one strong repo-native data point — `workers/cf-browser-fleet/src/__tests__/speech-session-pool.test.ts` prunes stale ready sessions whose DOs are already closed — but it's scoped to one subsystem; worth folding into item 6/11's generalization work (a shared `shared-utils` helper any handle-returning tool can call to validate a handle's session-epoch) rather than treating as a standalone gap.

### 5. Context, Memory & Continuity

## Overall read

Context, memory, and continuity is the most mature area in this audit by volume of working infrastructure, but nearly all of that maturity is borrowed rather than built: the durable per-project memory store at `/Users/buley/.claude/projects/-Users-buley-Documents-Code-monorepo/memory/MEMORY.md` (~150-217 topic files, `metadata.type` frontmatter distinguishing `project`/`user`/`feedback`/`reference`), background-task notification (`run_in_background` + `Monitor`), and deferred-tool lazy schema loading are all Claude Code harness features the repo happens to sit on top of, not things `a0`/gnosis/mcp-superserver engineered. Where the repo itself was supposed to add continuity machinery — compaction defense, crash resume, staleness detection, drift detection, cleanup — it consistently stops at "the platform incidentally helps" and never closes the loop with a repo-native mechanism. Concretely: no `PreCompact` hook exists anywhere (`.claude/settings.json` has no `hooks` key at all, repo or user level); `a0 flow`/`open-source/a0/src/cli.ts` has no `--resume` flag despite an append-only `.a0/control-plane/events.jsonl` that could drive one; memory entries have no staleness/TTL mechanism even though the exact pattern (`lastVerifiedAt`) already ships in `.a0/control-plane/workspace-control.qdoc` for build receipts; and confirmed-live disk leakage (`.a0/tmp/large-qa-datasets`, `.a0/tmp/moonshine-mesh-hotfix`, an orphaned `.claude/worktrees/agent-a56727561e88fcfbc`) shows cleanup is manual-only and already lapsing in practice.

## Verdict tally

- **MET**: 9 (User #46, User #41, User #47, User #48, Agent #48, Agent #43, Runtime #11, Runtime #12, Runtime #14)
- **PARTIAL**: 10 (Agent #41, Agent #44, Agent #42, Agent #45, Runtime #10, Runtime #16, Runtime #9, User #44, User #42, User #45)
- **MISSING**: 5 (Agent #47, Runtime #13, Runtime #15, Agent #46, User #43)
- **UNCLEAR**: 0

## Prioritized punch list

1. **No crash/resume path for `a0 flow` despite the data existing to drive one** (Agent #44, MISSING-adjacent/PARTIAL). `.a0/control-plane/events.jsonl` already logs sequenced `a0.task.start`/`a0.task.finish` per task, and `workspace-control.qdoc` snapshots status per project, but nothing in `open-source/a0/src/cli.ts` or `src/control-plane.ts` reads them to skip already-finished work (`grep`'d for `resume`/`--resume`/`resumeFrom`: zero hits). Recorded incidents (`a0 flow topo-commit hang`, `a0 flow OOM → gn flow push`) already show this costs real recovery time. **Fix**: wire the existing events.jsonl + qdoc snapshot into a real `--resume` flag on `a0 flow`/the task-graph runner so a rerun after a crash skips task keys already marked `finish` with `exitCode 0`.

2. **Memory facts have no staleness/re-verification mechanism** (Runtime #13, User #43, both MISSING). Entries carry only a soft date prefix; there's no TTL, flag, or gate, and one entry (`Clinamen Universal-Prediction FALSIFIED`) shows corrections currently happen ad hoc, not systematically. The fix pattern is already proven elsewhere in the repo twice over: `.a0/control-plane/workspace-control.qdoc`'s `lastVerifiedAt` stamp for build receipts, and `shared-api/src/senju/schemas.ts`'s `contradictionDensity`/cross-exam step from the ambient-fact-capture pipeline (built for AFFECTIVELY/Cissy end-user facts, not yet ported to the agent's own operational memory). **Fix**: stamp `MEMORY.md`/rollup entries with `lastVerifiedAt` and add a periodic skill that spot-checks referenced state (files exist, branch active, service deployed) and/or port the senju contradiction-flagging step to run against the memory store.

3. **No `PreCompact` hook anywhere — compaction defense is entirely incidental** (Runtime #9, Runtime #10, User #45, all PARTIAL). Grepped every `.claude/settings.json` (root, `open-source/gnosis`, `open-source/firefox`) for `PreCompact`/`SessionEnd`: only an unrelated `SessionStart` hook exists. Mandatory Task-tool usage (AGENTS.md B33a) means plan/blockedBy state survives compaction as a side effect of a platform feature the repo mandates, not by design — and there's zero evidence any repo priority (sovereign-tooling constraints, fork/race/fold discipline) gets re-injected after summarization. Cross-session continuity already works via memory files (e.g. `q3-mesh-revival.md`'s named "FLOOR" pickup point, an index line noting a file was "rescued 2026-07-04 during index compaction") — only the within-conversation case is undefended. **Fix**: add a `PreCompact` hook in `.claude/settings.json` that snapshots `TaskList` output/plan state and a condensed AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md digest into a scratch file so summarization has explicit unresolved-obligation state to work from.

4. **Ground-truth drift (branch/cwd/env) is a one-shot snapshot, not checked before risky calls** (Agent #42, Agent #46, both PARTIAL/MISSING). The harness env block and `gitStatus` block are injected once at session start and explicitly labeled "a snapshot in time, and will not update during the conversation." `a0 gn status` exists as a fast, branch-aware primitive but is pull-only and must be remembered — no hook or pre-flight check calls it automatically before state-mutating operations like `a0 flow`/`a0 xr deploy`. **Fix**: add a cheap repo-native pre-flight tool (extend `a0 gn status` or add an MCP `a0_context` tool returning `{cwd, branch, dirty-file count, relevant env vars}`) and a `PreToolUse` hook that calls it automatically before `a0 flow`/`a0 xr deploy`, rather than relying on the stale one-time snapshot.

5. **Scratch/cache cleanup is manual-only and already lapsing** (Agent #47, MISSING). `a0 clean`/`a0 clean disk --modules` (`open-source/a0/src/cli.ts`) must be explicitly invoked; live evidence of the gap: `.a0/tmp/large-qa-datasets` (a full separate git clone), `.a0/tmp/moonshine-mesh-hotfix`, and an orphaned `.claude/worktrees/agent-a56727561e88fcfbc` all sitting uncleaned right now. **Fix**: add a scheduled sweep (`CronCreate` or a `SessionStart`/`SessionEnd` hook alongside `open-source/gnosis/.claude/hooks/session-start.sh`) that runs `a0 clean disk --modules` and prunes `.a0/tmp/*` older than N days plus no-diff worktrees automatically.

6. **No task-id-scoped store for intra-task derived facts before compaction** (Agent #41, PARTIAL). AGENTS.md B33a mandates Task tools for every unit of work, and `TaskUpdate`'s `metadata` field could hold derived facts, but Task tools model work-item status, not arbitrary intermediate findings — no dedicated "facts" or "notes" store keyed to a task id was found in `apps/mcp-superserver` or `open-source/a0/src`. **Fix**: either document using `TaskUpdate metadata` explicitly for intra-task facts, or add a `scratchpad/<taskId>-facts.json` convention agents write to and re-read after compaction.

7. **No audit trail for mutations to shared state outside git** (Agent #45, PARTIAL). `.a0/control-plane/events.jsonl` genuinely audits build/task lifecycle events, and git/`a0 flow` covers source-tree mutations, but the memory store itself (`~/.claude/projects/.../memory/*.md`) lives outside git with only a one-time `originSessionId` frontmatter field — no trail of who edited what, when, or why on later edits. **Fix**: extend `.a0/control-plane/events.jsonl` (or add a sibling `.a0/audit/mutations.jsonl`) to log writer/session id, target resource, and reason whenever memory files or other non-git shared state is mutated, exposed via a `gn status --audit`-style query.

8. **No guaranteed "forget" mechanism for memory** (User #44, PARTIAL). Grepped AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md for "forget": no hits; no skill/command targets memory deletion. Memory is plain markdown so ad hoc deletion is technically possible, but the shipped ambient-fact-capture pipeline could silently re-derive and re-write a fact a user thought was gone, with no deny-list to stop it. **Fix**: add a `memory_forget <topic>` skill that deletes the topic file, removes its `MEMORY.md` index line, and writes a tombstone/deny-list entry the capture pipeline respects.

9. **No structured memory-review workflow for the user** (User #42, PARTIAL). Review/correction today means manually opening and hand-editing raw markdown under `~/.claude/projects/.../memory/`; no skill, confirm/diff UI, or "here's what's remembered, confirm or correct" surface exists. **Fix**: add a `/memory-review` skill/command listing recently written or stale entries with `type` + one-line summary for explicit user confirm/strike.

10. **Liquid-memory KV cache reuse is designed but currently disabled in prod** (Runtime #16, PARTIAL). `open-source/gnosis/distributed-inference-host/LIQUID_MEMORY_KV_SAVE.md` documents `/kv-session/save|load` built specifically to avoid full re-prefill, but it's mitigated off (`THOTH_LIQUID_MEMORY=0`) because the deployed `fat-station-cuda` binary lacks the routes; separately, no analogous discipline (stable tool-schema/system-prompt ordering) was found for Anthropic prompt-cache hits on the coding agent's own conversational context. **Fix**: finish the already-root-caused rebuild/redeploy of `fat-station-cuda` with `/kv-session/*` and re-enable `THOTH_LIQUID_MEMORY`; separately audit `apps/mcp-superserver`'s tool-schema assembly for stable ordering so Anthropic prompt-cache breakpoints aren't invalidated turn to turn.

11. **No token/turn budget enforcement for agent orchestration** (Runtime #15, MISSING). The only "budget" concept found is `a0 quality --budget`, a CI wall-clock time-box (`budgetMinutes`), unrelated to LLM token/turn ceilings; nothing in `a0` CLI or `apps/mcp-superserver` tracks cumulative tokens per turn/session. **Fix**: add a token-tracking primitive to mcp-superserver (which already sees every tool call/response) that accumulates tokens per session and warns/rejects further calls once a user-declared ceiling is hit.

### 6. Safety, Permissions & Trust Boundaries

## Overall Read

Safety in this repo is currently **policy-heavy, enforcement-light**. AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md contain genuinely strong, specific prose norms (B5 "ask about destruction," the git force-push/clean/reset bans, the shared-working-tree warning, the confirm-token convention on `control reconcile`), and a handful of real technical primitives exist in isolated corners of the codebase — UCAN capability scoping (`open-source/auth/src/ucanAuth.ts`), a fail-closed auth default in gnosis (`open-source/gnosis/src/auth/handlers.ts`), a 12-check "safety membrane" for CERA autonomous PRs (`shared-utils/src/agent-runtime/behaviors/cera-safety.ts`), MCP `destructiveHint` annotations, and per-command `--dry-run` flags scattered across `open-source/a0/src`. But none of these have been generalized or wired to the actual chokepoints that matter: there are **zero PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks anywhere in the repo** (one `SessionStart` hook in `open-source/gnosis/.claude/settings.json`, unrelated to safety), `.claude/settings.local.json` grants unscoped `"Bash"`/`WebFetch`/`WebSearch`/`Agent`/`Task` permissions that make per-call confirmation moot for whole classes of destructive action, and the product's own second tool-execution surface — `apps/mcp-superserver`'s `AdamAgent.callToolFn` — has no permission or capability check at all, independent of whatever the Claude Code harness enforces. Untrusted-content handling for fetched/scraped data is entirely undocumented and unimplemented. In short: the norms are well-written, the primitives exist in fragments, but almost nothing is a technical gate — safety currently depends on the model reading and honoring instructions rather than the system being unable to proceed without confirmation.

## Verdict Tally

- **MET:** 0
- **PARTIAL:** 21
- **MISSING:** 13
- **UNCLEAR:** 0
- **Total findings:** 34

## Prioritized Punch List

Ordered by estimated blast-radius reduction per fix — items that are root causes for several findings are surfaced first, since closing them collapses multiple gaps at once.

1. **No PreToolUse/PostToolUse hook exists anywhere to gate destructive actions deterministically** (Runtime #27, Runtime #32, Runtime #19, Runtime #25, User #25, User #27, Agent #56). This is the single biggest lever in the area: it underlies most of the other "policy not enforced" findings. Add a `PreToolUse` hook in root `.claude/settings.json` that (a) blocks/forces confirmation on destructive Bash patterns (`--force`, `-f\b`, `reset --hard`, `clean -f`, `rm -rf`) reusing the pattern list already written in `cera-safety.ts`, and (b) once the tool-schema fields below exist, checks the MCP tool's `destructiveHint`/`reversibility`/`requiresConfirmation` annotation and routes the decision through `open-source/auth/src/rules.ts`'s unused `evaluateRules()` (`{allowed, rulePath, reason}`) so a denial names exactly which rule fired, instead of the model's judgment being the only gate. Add a companion `PostToolUse` hook that scans Bash/tool stdout+stderr for secret-shaped strings and redacts them, generalizing the one-off `redact_ucan_in_argv` pattern in `open-source/gnosis/moonshine/src/agent_runtime.rs`.

2. **`.claude/settings.local.json` and `~/.claude/settings.json` pre-authorize entire classes of destructive action ambiently** (Runtime #26, Agent #51, User #32), and the global `defaultMode: "auto"` / `skipDangerousModePermissionPrompt: true` / `skipAutoPermissionPrompt: true` settings mean prompts are suppressed session-wide (Agent #55, Runtime #25). Replace the blanket `"Bash"`, `"WebFetch"`, `"WebSearch"`, `"Agent"`, `"Task"` entries with narrowly scoped, command/domain-prefixed patterns matching the existing `Bash(bun run:*)`-style entries already in the same file; use the `update-config` skill to regenerate a least-privilege `settings.local.json`. This is the fix that makes the norm in AGENTS.md B5 actually binding rather than honor-system.

3. **Destructive-tool schema fields (reversibility, confirmation, reason, safety classification) don't exist or aren't required**, so the hook in item 1 has nothing authoritative to check yet (Agent #49, Tool #34, Tool #39, Tool #37). Add to the shared Tool type in `open-source/mcp-framework/src/types.ts`: a required `reversibility: 'reversible' | 'irreversible' | 'unknown'` field (fail the generator/build if absent), a `requiresConfirmation: true` flag paired with `destructiveHint:true`, a required `reason: string` call-level input field threaded into tool logging, and a `safetyClassification: 'autonomous-safe' | 'supervised-only'` field gating `AdamAgent`'s chaining logic (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/adam-agent.ts`) to require a human turn before invoking supervised-only tools inside a loop. Document all of these in AGENTS.md as canonical queryable fields.

4. **The product's own tool-dispatch layer has no capability attenuation, no fail-closed default, and a second unguarded execution surface** (Tool #35, Tool #38, Runtime #19). `apps/mcp-superserver/src/skymesh-tools.ts`'s `buildHeaders()` forwards a caller-supplied `bearerDid` verbatim with no scoping, and `AdamAgent.callToolFn` invokes any registered tool directly with no permission check — a parallel surface the interactive-session chokepoint doesn't cover. Wire `@a0n/auth`'s `attenuateResource`/capability model into the superserver's outbound auth and tool dispatch, and port the `failClosed` default pattern from `open-source/gnosis/src/auth/handlers.ts` into `apps/mcp-superserver/src/runtime.ts` so unresolved permission/capability scope denies by default rather than falling through to allow.

5. **Untrusted external content (WebFetch/WebSearch/scraped data) is never flagged as distinct from user intent** — this exact gap was independently found three times (Tool #36, Agent #52, Runtime #29), which raises its priority despite being a single fix. Add an explicit paragraph to AGENTS.md instructing agents to treat fetched content as untrusted data, and if/when the superserver adds its own fetch-type tool, wrap the response in a clearly delimited `<untrusted-content source=...>` envelope, implemented once in a shared-utils or `apps/mcp-superserver` helper so every fetching tool passes through it.

6. **`a0 xr deploy`/`forge deploy` default to production with no confirmation step, and destructive a0 commands lack the confirm-token gate that already exists for one command** (User #30, Agent #50, User #29). `open-source/a0/src/cli.ts` (~3784-3795) defaults `env` to `'production'` with zero extra friction, while `runClean` (~5630-5661) and `reset` (~6235) execute immediately on flags parsed with no pre-check of `gn status`/`git status` for uncommitted work. Extract the existing `--confirm=<token>` gate from `control reconcile` into a shared helper, apply it to `a0 clean --scope=workspace`, `a0 reset`, and add a production-specific interactive confirmation to the deploy path, plus a pre-delete `gn status` guard before either clean/reset proceeds.

7. **No preview/simulate mode for destructive MCP tools or gnit's force-push, and no sandboxed mirror of production tool behavior for the MCP surface** (Agent #54, Runtime #30, Agent #62). `open-source/gnit/src/flow.rs`'s `git_push_forgo` executes with no preview step at all. Extract the per-command dry-run patterns already in `executor.ts`/`docker.ts`/`control-plane.ts` into a shared a0 preview contract, require any tool flagged destructive to implement it or explicitly declare "no preview available," and extend the `--env` pattern to the MCP superserver itself via a `SANDBOX_MODE` binding.

8. **No reversible-by-default posture: deletes are direct, and there's no undo path** (User #31, User #26). `open-source/a0/src/void-walk.ts:279` calls `rmSync(..., force: true)` directly with no quarantine step, and `gnit`'s command table (`open-source/gnit/src/main.rs`) has no revert/undo command at all. Add a rename-to-`.a0/trash/`-first quarantine step before permanent deletion in `void-walk.ts` (and codify "prefer rename-to-trash over rm" in AGENTS.md's B-series), and add a `gn undo`/`gn revert-last` command wrapping the last commit in a safe revert.

9. **Audit trail and rate/quota visibility are thin or unpopulated** (Tool #40, Agent #53, Tool #33). `AgentDelegationEnvelope.auditLabel` (`open-source/aeon-shell/src/kernel/types.ts:313`) is optional, free-form, and only ever populated in test fixtures — promote it to a required structured `{sessionId, taskId}` object populated by the orchestrator and add the equivalent field to the MCP tool-call envelope in `apps/mcp-superserver`. Separately, no budget/quota is proactively surfaced to an agent mid-task (only an on-demand `ForgoLogsClient.quota()` snapshot) — add a budget field to an a0 status surface polled before each step of a long-running flow. And no elevated-permission signal exists anywhere — emit one derived from `defaultMode`/`skipDangerousModePermissionPrompt` so tools can log when operating above baseline.

10. **Two parallel, un-reconciled sources of truth for "what's allowed"** (Runtime #17). The MCP tool matrix (`apps/mcp-superserver/src/generate-matrix-docs.ts`) and the Claude Code Bash-level permission allow-list are independently maintained and can drift. Extend `generate-matrix-docs.ts` to also emit the effective Claude Code permission surface so both are derived from one manifest.

### 7. Cost, Budget & Resource Economics

## Overall read

Cost/budget economics in this repo is almost entirely a **Cloudflare-infra-cost story**, not an **agentic-spend story**. `open-source/a0/src/cloudflare-cost.ts` is a genuinely solid piece of engineering — per-worker `$/mo` estimates from `wrangler.toml`, a `CF-BUDGET`/`CF-CRON-CAP` guardrail (`checkWranglerCostGuardrails`), a 30-day snapshot/sparkline, and `a0 show cost` reading `.a0/control-plane/events.jsonl`. But every one of those mechanisms measures **wall-clock time or static Cloudflare billing config**, not **tokens or dollars spent running the agent itself**. There is no field anywhere in `TaskCreate`/`TaskList`/`TaskGet`/`TaskUpdate` for cost, budget, or ceiling, and `CLAUDE.md`'s own Default Workflow ("fork independent work to sub-agents in parallel... Serial main-agent execution requires explicit dependency justification") actively biases toward the most expensive posture with no accompanying cost check. Compounding this, the one real cost **guardrail** that does exist (`checkWranglerCostGuardrails`) is wired into `a0 forge deploy` but **not** into `a0 xr deploy` — the command CLAUDE.md itself mandates as the only sanctioned deploy path — so the guardrail is bypassed by the very workflow the repo requires. The gap is not theoretical: two incident postmortems in memory (`cloud-run-gpu-cost-drain.md`, ~$2k; `cf-browser-fleet-paid-burn.md`, ~1000 paid browser-hours) show real money burned and discovered only via invoices/anomalous commits, never via automated tracking, spike detection, or an idle-resource sweep.

## Verdict tally

| Verdict | Count |
|---|---|
| MET | 2 |
| PARTIAL | 13 |
| MISSING | 10 |
| UNCLEAR | 0 |
| **Total** | **25** |

## Prioritized punch list

1. **No shared LLM token/$ budget pool for agent fanout** (Runtime #49, MISSING) — `TaskCreate`/`TaskUpdate` have no budget/cost/ceiling field; grepping `open-source/a0/src`, `apps/mcp-superserver/src`, `open-source/gnosis` for `token.?budget|budget.?ceiling|budget.?pool` returns nothing. *Recommendation:* add a `budget` object (token or $ ceiling, consumed-so-far) to the metadata `TaskCreate`/`TaskUpdate` accept; decrement it on sub-agent completion via a control-plane hook alongside the existing `events.jsonl` journal, refusing new spawns once exhausted. This is foundational — nearly every other gap below (preflight estimates, dashboards, per-project attribution) needs this data source to exist first.

2. **`a0 show cost` tracks wall-clock duration, not $ or tokens** (User #38, PARTIAL) — `computeCost`/`printCostSummary` (`open-source/a0/src/show.ts:646,879`) already read `.a0/control-plane/events.jsonl` and print an aggregate on request — the right *pattern* — but there is no spend/token field in the event stream at all, for Anthropic or Cloudflare. *Recommendation:* append estimated $ or token count on each `a0.task.finish`/tool-call event and extend `computeCost`/`printCostSummary` to report it, yielding `a0 show spend` almost for free from plumbing that already exists. Highest-leverage quick win since the harness is already there.

3. **The mandated deploy path skips the cost guardrail** (Operator #8 and Operator #2, both PARTIAL) — `checkWranglerCostGuardrails` (`open-source/a0/src/cloudflare-cost.ts:1235`) genuinely blocks `a0 forge deploy` on critical violations, but `a0 xr deploy` — the command CLAUDE.md requires for **all** deploys — calls `wrangler deploy` via `runXRanger`/`handleDeploy` (`open-source/x-ranger/src/cli.ts:2218`) after only a config-divergence preflight, never the cost guardrail. *Recommendation:* wire `auditCloudflare({strict:true})`'s exit code directly into `a0 xr deploy` (`cli.ts` subcommand `==='deploy'`, ~line 3773) so a budget-exceeding deploy is blocked by default rather than requiring a separate, easy-to-skip `a0 audit cloudflare --strict` invocation.

4. **No preflight cost/token estimate before multi-agent fanout** (Agent #63, User #33, Runtime #51, Runtime #55 — all MISSING/PARTIAL, one connected cluster) — nothing prints an estimate before a `TaskCreate` fanout or fork/race/fold spawn; the only estimator in the repo (`cloudflare-cost.ts`) prices Cloudflare infra, not agent runs, and isn't invoked at spawn time. *Recommendation:* add a preflight estimator (`a0 estimate fanout <n>` or a wrapper around the Task-tool invocation) that computes planned-agent-count × avg-tokens-per-agent (sourced from #2's spend tally once it exists) and prints/requires explicit ack above a threshold; extend it to print a marginal "agent N+1 costs ~X" line as fanout grows (Runtime #55), reusing the cost-table pattern already established in `cloudflare-cost.ts`.

5. **CLAUDE.md defaults to maximum fanout instead of cheapest-sufficient** (User #35, PARTIAL) — the Default Workflow bullet makes heavy parallel fanout the standing default and requires justification to stay serial, the inverse of "default to cheapest, scale only when asked"; no `haiku`/`effort level`/`cheapest` default policy exists anywhere in AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md. *Recommendation:* amend the Default Workflow bullet to require the cheapest-sufficient approach by default and scope fork/race/fold to proven-independent subtasks or explicit user asks for that scale, rather than making heavy fanout the standing posture. This is a documentation-only fix but is the root cause driving unnecessary spend across items 1 and 4.

6. **No idle/stuck-agent watchdog** (Runtime #53, MISSING) — `TaskStop` only kills a task manually; no reaper/stale-agent/idle-timeout logic exists in `open-source/a0/src`, and no `hooks` key exists in `.claude/settings.local.json`. Corroborated directly by `rogue-cloud-routines-disabled.md`: four cloud-scheduled routines ran unattended and mutated infra via raw `wrangler`, caught only by manual discovery on 2026-06-16. *Recommendation:* add a heartbeat/last-activity timestamp to background task state (extending `.a0/control-plane/events.jsonl`) and a periodic sweep (`a0 control reap`, alongside existing `a0 control autoscale`/`reconcile`) that `TaskStop`s or `RemoteTrigger`-disables anything idle past a threshold.

7. **No automated idle-infra sweep or cron-tightness floor** (Operator #5, User #37 — both PARTIAL) — the only checks are deploy-time preflights (`config-divergence.ts`) and a manual, human-triggered `gcloud run services list` audit documented in AGENTS.md, run reactively "after any Cloud Run changes." Live production crons already run as tight as `*/1 * * * *` (`apps/dashrelay-app/wrangler.toml:95`, `apps/halo-community/wrangler.toml:29`) and `* * * * *` (`open-source/aeon-solarium/wrangler.toml:16`), directly implicated in the ~$2k GPU drain and ~1000 paid browser-hour incidents. *Recommendation:* (a) wrap the existing gcloud audit command in a scheduled `RemoteTrigger`/cron routine that runs daily and pages the operator on any non-zero min-instances/warm pool; (b) add a lint/preflight (extending `x-ranger`'s config-divergence check or the Cloudflare cost audit) that flags any `wrangler.toml` cron tighter than a configurable floor (5-15 min) unless explicitly annotated as intentional.

8. **No cross-provider spend dashboard or per-project/product attribution** (Operator #1, Operator #3 — both PARTIAL) — `cloudflare-cost.ts` gives real per-worker Cloudflare $/mo attribution and a cost sparkline, but nothing tracks Anthropic API spend, Cloud Run billing, or ties LLM spend to a product label (AFFECTIVELY, Aeon, Dash, etc.); `a0 show cost` is compute-duration only. *Recommendation:* build `a0 show spend --all-providers` merging the Cloudflare estimate, a Cloud Run pull (`gcloud run services list`/billing export), and an Anthropic usage-API pull into one table via the existing `printCostSummary`/`printJson` pattern in `show.ts`; tag agent/task invocations with a project label and persist per-label totals the way `cf-cost-history` already does for Cloudflare.

9. **No shared outbound rate-limit ledger across independent agent processes** (Runtime #52, MISSING) — every rate-limit handling site found (`open-source/gnosis/src/knotgraft-client.ts:204`, `open-source/a0/src/seo.ts:506-536`, `apps/edge-web-app/src/worker.ts:4805-4855`) is a local single-caller retry/backoff loop; none writes 429/remaining-quota state anywhere a second agent could read before making its own call. *Recommendation:* add a KV/D1-backed shared rate-limit ledger keyed by provider/endpoint, written on every 429 and consulted before dispatch, so concurrent fanned-out agents don't independently hammer the same rate-limited endpoint.

10. **Spend-trend/spike detection exists in code but is dead and too coarse** (Operator #4, MISSING) — `printCostSparkline` (`cloudflare-cost.ts:1516`) has all its `console.log` lines commented out (emits nothing), operates at daily granularity, tracks a static wrangler.toml estimate rather than actual billed spend, and has no threshold/spike (Nx-vs-baseline) check. *Recommendation:* re-enable the sparkline output, move it to hourly granularity, and add an explicit spike-detection rule (e.g., flag when current-period cost > N× trailing average) analogous to the `CF-BUDGET` guardrail already in the same file.

11. **No free-tier-vs-overage distinction anywhere** (Operator #7, MISSING) — AGENTS.md only carries a written warning ("$1,000+ bills from AI services... free tier becomes paid after usage caps"); `cloudflare-cost.ts` computes flat estimates with no included-quota/overage split for D1/KV/R2/Workers requests, and nothing distinguishes included vs. billed usage for Anthropic, Workers AI, or Cloud Run. *Recommendation:* add a free-tier-threshold table to `cloudflare-cost.ts` so `estimatedMonthlyCostUsd` reports $0 up to included quota and only counts overage, surfaced distinctly in `a0 show cf-cost`.

12. **Budget-driven scaling pattern is proven but not generalized** (User #34, Runtime #54 — both PARTIAL) — `a0 quality --budget=<minutes>` (`open-source/a0/src/cli.ts:4624-4663`) is a real, enforced example of computing fleet/target size from remaining budget rather than a fixed count, but it's scoped to CI test/lint target selection by wall-clock minutes only — not agent fleet size, loop depth, or $/token budget. *Recommendation:* generalize the `--budget` selection algorithm into a shared helper usable by the agent-spawn/fork-race-fold path, and add an equivalent `a0 flow --budget=<usd|tokens>` flag that hard-stops further sub-agent work once a ceiling is reached.

13. **Model/effort transparency and lightweight-path affordances are user-invisible or not repo-native** (User #36, User #40 — both PARTIAL) — the `/api/agent/chat/models` admin model-override panel (`apps/edge-web-app/src/client/pages/halogram/HalogramHeader.tsx`) is gated behind `isAdmin = false` by default, so regular users never see which model/tier served their request (tier/subscription-driven, not difficulty-driven, per `worker.ts` comments). Separately, the only scale-down affordance found (effort-`level` on the code-review/security-review skills) is a harness-provided Claude Code skill, not something this repo engineered (`find .claude/skills` lists only `hft-trade-log-diagnose`, `kenoma-about-app`, `tauri-native-app` as project-owned). *Recommendation:* surface model/tier used in the response UI for all users (not just admins), and add a repo-native lightweight-mode flag to the fork/race/fold orchestration path itself rather than relying solely on the generic harness skill's effort levels.

### 8. Observability, Audit & Feedback

## Overall read

The repo has decent *local* observability primitives — a real append-only event log (`.a0/control-plane/events.jsonl`), explicit truncation flags on at least one tool (`read_file`), a working `--verbose`/failures-only default in `a0 run`, honest error-message passthrough (no paraphrasing) in both the MCP superserver and the CLI, and genuine dry-run support on 15+ CLI subcommands plus MCP deploy tools. Structured (`--json` / `format: json`) output also exists on both surfaces.

But almost everything that would make this observable *to a human operator*, rather than just replayable by a build-task grep, is missing. There is no unified alert channel, no scheduled digest, no login/account-anomaly detection, and no push-based health canary — cost and security incidents documented in the user's own memory (`cf-browser-fleet-paid-burn.md`, `cloud-run-gpu-cost-drain.md`) were both discovered reactively, which is exactly what a mature version of this area would prevent. Equally, the loudest policy in the repo — "SOVEREIGN TOOLING IS MANDATORY" — has zero mechanical enforcement (no PreToolUse hook, no husky gate), and it has already been violated in production by a since-disabled cloud routine. And the agent-facing tool-calling runtime itself (`agentic-chat.ts`) has no structured journal at all — the one real event log in the repo belongs to the a0 build-task-graph, not to LLM agent tool calls.

Net: strong scaffolding at the CLI/build-task layer, thin-to-absent coverage at the operator-alerting layer and the agent-runtime-journaling layer — the two places where silent failure is most expensive.

## Verdict tally

| Verdict | Count |
|---|---|
